paragraphs.body: My brother and I are both mixed Chinese and White, my mom being Cantonese and originally from China and my dad being White and growing up a city. A few years ago, while I was eating dinner with my family, my brother began discussing how he fit in at school. He began by talking about growing up in America and being in touch with American culture more so than Chinese yet still looking more Chinese than White with black hair and darker skin. He talked about not feeling like he fit in with the Chinese kids who grew up surrounded by Chinese tradition. He said how he felt as if he wasn’t “Chinese enough” to fit in comfortably with that group but enough to not fully fit in with the White kids either. He ended by saying how he honestly was confused about what he identified as; would growing up in the U.S. and being raised catholic be enough to identify as White? Is looking Asian-ish and traveling multiple times to China enough to identify as Chinese? Or should he just settle with the awkward in-between mixed thing that can be the best and worst of both worlds?

annotations.text: would growing up in the U.S. and being raised catholic be enough to identify as White? Is looking Asian-ish and traveling multiple times to China enough to identify as Chinese? Or should he just settle with the awkward in-between mixed thing that can be the best and worst of both worlds?

paragraphs.body: What am I? If I both am and am not, I am Chinese and Irish, I am Chinese and Irish, If I both am and am not, What am I? Before my brother said this I hadn’t thought about the topic. While I thought about it, I considered the language I grew up with, not necessarily the literal language spoken in my house since I only speak English, but instead the language of my family. I think the language, including words, phrases, sayings, adlibs, and really anything unique to your home, can define how a person identifies. The first language I want to write about is the White things on my dad’s side. My dad grew up Irish Catholic, he'd do the standard Sunday Church every week, his father was in the Navy, and during the summer my dad would do flooring for the shipyard down in a city. When my brother and I were born we were both baptized Catholic, we used to both attend church every Sunday just like our dad did when he was young. I remember vividly attending CCD on Wednesdays to learn more about the holy book and Catholicism, we would have to memorize prayers like the Hail Mary, the Apostles Creed, and the Lord’s Prayer which the teachers always told us had some secret second part that only the priest knew. One of the big things from this language was Grace, my dad taught us how to say Grace and now it’s stuck in my head forever.

annotations.text: I hadn’t thought about the topic.
annotations.comment: I'm interested in this response. Why had it never occurred to you? I think there's something to discover here :)

annotations.text: My dad grew up Irish Catholic, he'd do the standard Sunday Church every week, his father was in the Navy, and during the summer my dad would do flooring for the shipyard down in a city.
If I both am and am not, What am I?

paragraphs.body: “Bless us, O’ lord, for these Thy gifts, which we are about to receive from Thy bounty, through Christ our lord. Amen.”

I’ve probably heard this prayer over fifty thousand times, and it has become a part of who I am. One of the first things I did when I arrived at Pitt was visit the St. Paul Cathedral. I am not religious anymore and I never connected fully with the tradition, but it was still such a huge part of my childhood that I still felt at home attending mass. It was weird that after not being to church for almost 5 years I knew when to stand or kneel, I knew most of the prayers from memory, I still remembered the overall structure of mass, and I even remembered how to receive Holy Communion. Being able to navigate church after so many years on autopilot showed how integral this part of my life was even if I haven’t been in touch with my holy side very much recently.

annotations.text: I’ve probably heard this prayer over fifty thousand times, and it has become a part of who I am.
annotations.comment: What do you mean, specifically? Has it defined your values? Tie you to home and family? Something else? Let's talk :)

annotations.text: Being able to navigate church after so many years on autopilot showed how integral this part of my life was even if I haven’t been in touch with my holy side very much recently.
annotations.comment: I'm interested! We might explore religion in your life as a potentially meaningfully friction :)

paragraphs.body: The next language that my family uses is all the Chinese stuff. My mom would always say Chinese phrases to my brother and I but I honestly wouldn’t even count them using the language Chinese in our house. Since neither of us ever fully learned to speak we had no clue what these phrases meant literally, but after putting two and two together we began to figure out what they meant. The phrase I probably heard the most growing up was “Zou la, zou la, kuai dian!” which translates to “Let’s go, let’s go, hurry up!” I honestly didn’t know the real translation of the phrase before I started working on this essay because I always just related being late with the phrase.

annotations.text: “Zou la, zou la, kuai dian!” which translates to “Let’s go, let’s go, hurry up!”

paragraphs.body: Similarly to Grace, this is another thing that I grew up hearing basically my entire life. I would hear this when I had to wake up for school, pack for a vacation, get in the car to go to a doctor’s appointment, get ready for church, get in the family photo at Christmas, and so many other situations where I just wasn’t on time. Along with hearing it said directly to me, I’d hear my mom say it a bunch to my brother as well which solidified the meaning in my head.

Another phrase that I heard a lot from my mom was “Sik fan, sik fan.” This phrase is in Cantonese which is a specific dialect from the southern parts of China like Guangzhou and Hong Kong. The phrase translates to “Eat rice, eat rice.” but my mom usually just uses it as “Eat, Eat!” A big part of Cantonese culture is the food, they love huge dinners, hot pots, and piles of plates on the table for basically any meal. When I went to China every night there would be a huge dinner at a big round table with a turntable full of dishes and we would all just sit there and eat for hours. My mom told me that if I looked away from an empty bowl, by the time I look back it’ll probably be full again. It sounds pretty crazy, but it honestly happened at almost every dinner we ate, my family over there doesn’t get to see me as much so along with the normal cultural aspects they also just wanted me to try so many of these different dishes that my bowl would just always be full of anything.

annotations.text: My mom told me that if I looked away from an empty bowl, by the time I look back it’ll probably be full again.
annotations.comment: Fascinating...I wonder if there might be a metaphor to explore here, too...

paragraphs.body: These phrases and experiences are things I relate with my childhood and self yet still, I feel they are more emulations of my parents’ childhoods instead of things that I found myself connecting with personally. One of the languages that I think is a perfect example of this is the Eagles. I love football and no matter how awful the Eagles get they’ll always be my team. I remember growing up watching Eagles games at my grandparents’ house eating hoagies and cheesesteaks with my family gathered around the TV. It’s one of the experiences I relate deeply to my childhood and something I still do. Just a few weeks ago when I was home for fall break, I spent a day at my grandparent’s house watching the Birds stumble their way to a victory. Even when I’m not home, I always watch the Eagles game every week no matter what, even if I’m in the Cathedral of Learning studying for a Midterm, I gotta support the Birds. Things like, “GO BIRDS!”, “E-A-G-L-E-S, EAGLES!” and even the fight song are things that I’ve always included in my language at home and are just a part of my language in general. I honestly think that one of the reasons I haven’t felt homesick at Pitt is related to seeing so many Eagles fans here. One of the funniest things for me was after the first Pitt game versus Kent State when somebody started an Eagles chant in the ramp which made me feel right at home. Sometimes it’s complicated to be neither white nor Chinese fully. When I first got to the University of Pittsburgh, I went to the Chinese club and the Irish club here, and I didn’t really feel like a part of either. My name is a language in itself that makes me not quite fit in. Signing my name Aaron O’Sullivan at the Chinese club didn’t seem to fit. My middle name, Wang, creates a mixed impression since it’s in Chinese. And then signing my name and going to the Irish club and not being a pale redhead kid. However, when I look at these diverse languages and experiences that I’ve been able to experience I have a certain sense of pride in being Waisin. I love identifying as both Chinese and American and I’m proud to have experiences from both cultures. It’s nice to have experiences from two such diverse cultures, to have different things to share with people, and also things to relate to. I have a lot of white friends, and I like to take them to Chinese restaurants, but because my dad is Irish and white we share a lot of things in common. I don’t mind being the “diversity hire” of the group as they joke, and I don’t mind being just another white kid in the fold.

annotations.text: feel they are more emulations of my parents’ childhoods instead of things that I found myself connecting with personally.

paragraphs.body: So, in response to my brother’s point at the dinner table, I would say, what’s so wrong with being different? We could’ve fit in more by being born full Chinese, attending Chinese school, learning the Violin, and hanging out with all the Chinese kids, or were born full White and continued to attend church every week, joined the Irish club, and ate dry unspiced food for the rest of our life. But we would end up missing so many experiences from either side, who knows if I would ever attend a mass at a Catholic church if I were born Chinese, or if I would ever be able to travel to the Countryside in China and drink tea with family in a literal stone house. These are the things that make me who I and I'm proud to have had these experiences. So, I’m fine settling with the awkward in-between mixed thing because it really is the best of both worlds.
